title: Exposing One Service Privately
description:
## Service, Not Network, Exposure

A SaaS team operates a backend API behind a **Network Load Balancer** in their own VPC. They want **many consumer VPCs across different AWS accounts** to call this single API privately. Hard requirements:

- Consumers must reach **only this one service**, not the rest of the provider's VPC or its CIDR range.
- Traffic must **stay on the AWS private network** (never traverse the public internet).
- It must scale to a large, possibly overlapping set of consumer accounts without coordinating IP ranges.

What should the provider use?

options.text: Publish the service with **AWS PrivateLink**: create a **VPC endpoint service** in front of the NLB; consumers create **interface VPC endpoints** to it in their own VPCs.
Set up **VPC peering** between the provider VPC and every consumer VPC so they can reach the API.
Attach all VPCs to a **Transit Gateway** and let consumers route to the provider's NLB.
Put the NLB on a **public** subnet with a public IP and restrict access with security groups per consumer.

explanation: **AWS PrivateLink** is designed precisely for exposing a *single service* privately to many consumers. The provider fronts the service with an NLB and creates a **VPC endpoint service**; each consumer provisions an **interface VPC endpoint** (an ENI in their own subnet) to connect. Consumers can reach **only that endpoint** — not the provider's broader VPC or CIDR — traffic stays on the AWS backbone, and because the connection is unidirectional service exposure, **overlapping CIDRs between accounts don't matter**. Option B (VPC peering) exposes whole **CIDR ranges** at the network level (violating least-exposure), is **non-transitive**, fails with overlapping CIDRs, and doesn't scale to many accounts. Option C (Transit Gateway) is network-level routing, not service-level exposure, and similarly exposes more than the one service and breaks on CIDR overlap. Option D puts the service on the **public internet**, directly violating the private-connectivity requirement.

hints.title: Service-level vs. network-level
hints.body: Peering and TGW connect networks (CIDRs). PrivateLink exposes one specific service via an endpoint, hiding everything else.

hints.title: Overlapping CIDRs are a tell
hints.body: If many accounts may have overlapping IP ranges and you only need to share one service, PrivateLink sidesteps the IP-coordination problem entirely.
